theses.summary: Mid-cap tech growth stocks are at or below 2018 trough multiples despite better fundamentals, creating a compelling entry point
theses.detail: Baker argues that software and internet multiples have round-tripped to 2018 levels, but the underlying businesses are growing faster and have better margins than in 2018. In 2018, the 10-year was at 3% and the market was deep into a tightening cycle. Today, companies are at or below those trough multiples with better fundamentals, making the opportunity set more attractive than it appears.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker explicitly states he is 'as bullish as I've ever been on these names that are down 60% to 70%' and describes buying weakness actively.
theses.quote_fragment: as bullish as I've ever been on these names that are down 60% to 70%

theses.summary: Supply-chain-driven goods inflation is transitory and will normalize; wage inflation is the real risk to watch
theses.detail: Baker distinguishes two inflation components: (1) supply-chain/goods inflation, which he is highly confident will normalize due to massive supply response (Amazon CapEx doubled in 2 years vs prior 20, TSMC 2022 CapEx multiples of 2019), slowing economy, and shift from goods to services spending; (2) wage inflation, which is genuinely uncertain and structurally dangerous if persistent. He views goods inflation as near-certain to reverse but is explicitly humble about wages.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says he is 'so relaxed' about supply-chain inflation and provides extensive data. On wages he explicitly says 'who knows' and flags it as the key risk.
theses.quote_fragment: I am so relaxed about that...capitalism is amazing at solving problems

theses.summary: Persistent wage inflation would be severely negative for all equities, but tech would be relatively better positioned
theses.detail: Using Buffett's 1970s framework, Baker explains that inflation inflates the asset base and depresses ROE/ROIC, compressing equity returns. However, modern tech companies are asset-light with highest ROICs, massive pricing power, and lowest employees per dollar of gross profit — the inverse of 1970s tech. So while all equities suffer from wage inflation, tech should outperform on a relative basis.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker frames this as a conditional view ('if wage inflation is here to stay') and explicitly caveats with humility about macro forecasting.
theses.quote_fragment: tech companies today, they're super asset-like...highest ROICs...massive pricing power

theses.summary: Software is the 'consumer staple of tech' — fundamentals will be most resilient in a slowing economy
theses.detail: Baker is 'really, really bullish on software' and views it as having the most stable relative fundamentals in a slowdown. Unlike internet companies suffering COVID hangovers, software has no comparable demand normalization headwind. CIO surveys and JP Morgan commentary support accelerating cloud spend in 2022. Baker has a strong bias toward free-cash-flow-generating software names, particularly those with 2-4% FCF yields looking to 2023.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker uses superlatives ('really, really bullish'), cites JP Morgan commentary, CIO surveys, and explicitly contrasts with internet weakness.
theses.quote_fragment: software is the consumer staple of tech...best relative fundamentals

theses.summary: Infrastructure software is preferred over application software due to structural risk from hyperscaler competition
theses.detail: Baker has a 'big bias towards infrastructure companies relative to application software.' Application software companies without deep technology are at risk as cloud vendors make it easier for enterprises to build their own apps. Google was first to embrace third-party infrastructure software providers as a channel; Azure followed. AWS is the last holdout. Baker believes the world of software will not become an oligopoly of just the three hyperscalers.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker states this as a structural belief extending to terminal value, not just near-term positioning.
theses.quote_fragment: big bias towards infrastructure companies relative to application software

theses.summary: SMB-focused application software is relatively shielded from hyperscaler build-your-own risk
theses.detail: Baker notes that SMBs cannot roll their own apps on AWS, making SMB-focused application software companies structurally protected from the threat that large enterprises will build their own applications using cloud primitives. This is a relative preference within application software.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker states this as a directional bias ('I have a bias towards ones that serve SMBs') without naming specific companies.
theses.quote_fragment: SMBs are not going to be able to roll their own apps in AWS

theses.summary: AI has structurally changed cybersecurity, enabling scale advantages for the first time and allowing companies to break through the historical $15-20B market cap ceiling
theses.detail: Baker was previously structurally bearish on cybersecurity because attackers would optimize against any dominant player, VCs would fund competitors, and scale hurt rather than helped. AI-driven cybersecurity reverses this: if models are learning, scale becomes an advantage. This is why, for the first time, cybersecurity companies have broken through the $20B market cap barrier and continue growing.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker describes this as a structural change in his view, contrasting with his prior bearish stance, and points to observable market cap outcomes as evidence.
theses.quote_fragment: AI has changed that...scale is good for you...for the first time ever

theses.summary: Semiconductor industry faces a near-term inventory cycle downturn despite long-term structural demand upgrade
theses.detail: Baker is 'relatively cautious on semis' near-term. The industry faces: (1) an inventory cycle unwind as economy slows and PCE shifts from goods to services; (2) new capacity coming online that could worsen the downturn. However, long-term, the industry has shifted from ~2x nominal GDP growth to ~3x nominal GDP growth due to AI compute intensity and EV/AV semiconductor content. The supply structure remains highly consolidated (monopolies/duopolies in every subsector).
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker explicitly states he is 'as cautious as I've been on semiconductors in a long time right now' and provides detailed structural reasoning for both the near-term caution and long-term bullishness.
theses.quote_fragment: relatively cautious on semis...massive inventory cycle...economy slowing

theses.summary: Semiconductor industry has structurally shifted to ~3x nominal GDP growth rate from ~2x, driven by AI and EV/AV content
theses.detail: AI training requires exponentially more compute (quality doubles with every 10x increase in training data per a Microsoft research paper). AI is eating software. EVs and eventually AVs dramatically increase semiconductor content per vehicle. These two forces have permanently shifted the semiconductor industry's growth multiplier relative to GDP upward.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker cites specific research (Microsoft paper on AI scaling), frames this as a structural long-term view, and uses it as the foundation for his long-term bullishness on semis post-cycle.
theses.quote_fragment: used to grow at 2x nominal GDP to one that probably now is a 3x nominal GDP grower

theses.summary: Internet advertising and e-commerce companies face a double headwind of COVID hangover and economic slowdown; search is the exception
theses.detail: Baker expects broad fundamental weakness in internet names (e-commerce and advertising) due to COVID normalization pulling demand back below trend and GDP sensitivity in a slowing economy. Search is the exception because it is levered to the resurgence in services spending. Subscription internet companies are more similar to software in their resilience.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker references 'reasonable telemetry' from app downloads, web traffic, and credit card data, and explicitly distinguishes search from the rest.
theses.quote_fragment: real legitimate fundamental weakness in almost all of the internet names, maybe with the exception of search

theses.summary: The metaverse is real and coming, but today's true metaverses are video games (Roblox, Fortnite, World of Warcraft, Destiny, Minecraft)
theses.detail: Baker believes the metaverse trend is genuine but overhyped in branding. He identifies existing video game platforms as the real metaverses where commerce happens and people live digital lives. He is excited about PlayStation VR and Apple's upcoming headset. He believes mobile OS platforms (Apple/Android) have profound advantages in the metaverse transition as VR/AR converge into XR.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker expresses genuine belief but acknowledges the term has become overdone and frames his views as long-term directional rather than near-term investment calls.
theses.quote_fragment: the only real metaverse is today...video games...real metaverses where commerce happens

theses.summary: Microsoft's acquisition of Activision is a strategic metaverse/gaming platform play, and Microsoft has the most compelling AR/VR asset collection of any company
theses.detail: Microsoft's acquisitions of Bethesda, Activision, and Minecraft, combined with the Hololens military contract and Windows PC gaming dominance, give it the most pure AR/VR revenue of any company. Baker views this as Microsoft building a comprehensive gaming/metaverse platform and slowly recapturing PC gaming from Steam.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker frames this as an analytical observation about Microsoft's strategic positioning rather than an explicit investment recommendation.
theses.quote_fragment: almost certainly going to have the most pure AR VR revenue of any company

theses.summary: Large internet/cloud/advertising platforms are levered royalties on global GDP with a structural tailwind from the rising share of revenue that companies spend with them
theses.detail: Baker describes a cohort analysis: companies founded 50 years ago spend ~2% of revenue with modern internet platforms; companies founded in the last 3 years may spend 50%+ of revenue with them. Every year, the world moves toward these platforms. Combined with semiconductors growing at 3x GDP, these platforms may collectively grow at an even higher multiple of GDP.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker presents this as a structural framework he uses, with illustrative numbers he acknowledges are approximate.
theses.quote_fragment: every year, the world moves towards them

theses.summary: Private market valuations will cool if public market weakness persists 6-9 months; public multiples are the ultimate reality for private valuations
theses.detail: Baker argues the illiquidity premium in private markets is structural and here to stay (the entire system wants less frequent marks), but public market weakness that persists will eventually force private valuations lower. He notes this is a consistent historical relationship over his 20 years in venture. The lag works both ways — private valuations are slow to adjust up and down.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker states 'I promise you, this weakness persists, private valuations will cool off' and cites 20 years of consistent observation.
theses.quote_fragment: public multiples are the ultimate reality...private valuations will cool off

theses.summary: The value-over-growth rotation thesis is weaker today than post-2001 because there is no comparable demand impulse (China/BRICs industrialization) to drive value earnings
theses.detail: Baker argues that value's outperformance from 2001-2008 was driven not just by multiple expansion but by a massive earnings impulse from China's WTO entry and industrialization creating step-function demand for commodities, materials, and industrials. No comparable demand impulse exists today, making a sustained value rotation less likely even in a higher inflation environment.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker presents this as a structural analytical point but acknowledges uncertainty about the inflation/wage environment.
theses.quote_fragment: I just don't see any comparable demand impulse for a lot of these traditional smokestack industries

theses.summary: Free cash flow generation is the key differentiator within growth tech in the current environment; market is beginning to price this distinction
theses.detail: Baker has a 'big line in the sand' around free cash flow generation. Companies at 2-4% FCF yield looking to 2023 are fundamentally different from cash-burning peers. He notes the market began differentiating on this basis in the week prior to the interview, moving away from the prior 'correlation one' sell-offs where everything over 10-15x sales fell equally.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker describes this as a 'big line in the sand' and observes real-time market behavior confirming the differentiation.
theses.quote_fragment: big line in the sand for me...two, two and a half, 3% free cash flow yield looking out to 23

theses.summary: Apple is likely to enter search, and mobile OS platforms will collide with other tech ecosystems as the metaverse develops
theses.detail: Baker believes Apple will eventually do search. As VR/AR converge into XR and the metaverse develops, mobile OS control becomes a profound advantage. This will cause ecosystem collisions between companies that previously didn't compete directly.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker uses 'eventually' and frames this as a directional belief rather than a near-term catalyst.
theses.quote_fragment: Apple is going to do search...they're eventually going to do it

explicit_recommendations.target: Mid-cap tech growth stocks with positive free cash flow, down 60-70% from highs
explicit_recommendations.reasoning: Multiples at or below 2018 trough levels despite better fundamentals; businesses growing faster with better margins; relative fundamentals should drive relative performance over 12-18 months; FCF generation provides downside protection

Structural preference over application software due to hyperscaler competition risk for app companies; infrastructure software benefits from cloud adoption without the build-your-own threat; AWS is the last holdout in embracing third-party infrastructure providers
Massive inventory cycle likely to unwind as economy slows, PCE shifts from goods to services, and new capacity comes online; when semis miss, they miss big (reference to Q4 2018)
Double headwind of COVID demand normalization and GDP-sensitive business models in a slowing economy; telemetry from app downloads, web traffic, and credit card data supports fundamental weakness
Only internet subsector levered to resurgence in services spending post-COVID; shielded from COVID hangover that affects e-commerce and social advertising

sector_rankings.reasoning: Application software without deep technology is at risk from hyperscalers enabling enterprises to build their own apps; infrastructure software rides on top of cloud giants and benefits from their growth without facing the same competitive threat
Software has no COVID hangover, more stable recurring revenues, and is less GDP-sensitive; internet names face double headwind of normalization and economic slowdown

Multiples already at 2018 trough levels with better fundamentals; no comparable demand impulse exists for value stocks as existed with China/BRICs in 2001-2008; tech is asset-light with highest ROICs in an inflationary environment
Long-term structural demand upgrade to 3x GDP growth from AI and EV/AV; but near-term inventory cycle unwind and capacity additions create significant downside risk

risk_warnings.detail: Inventory cycle unwind is likely as economy slows and PCE shifts from goods to services; all the capacity being brought on could worsen the downturn; companies miss big when they miss (Q4 2018 cited as example); tourists in the sector are pricing semis like software

risk_warnings.target: Application software companies without deep technology
risk_warnings.detail: As cloud vendors make it easier to build custom applications, enterprises may increasingly build their own rather than buy from application software vendors; this risk is most acute for companies with traditional enterprise go-to-market and no true technology moat

Private markets do not mark to market, creating an illiquidity premium that masks true valuation risk; if public market weakness persists 6-9 months, private valuations will be forced to correct; public multiples are the ultimate reality

Persistent wage inflation would compress ROE/ROIC across all equities per Buffett's 1970s framework; the 10-year could go well above consensus estimates of 2.5-3.25% if wage inflation is structural, invalidating all current valuation frameworks

COVID pulled demand significantly above trend; real personal consumption expenditures show goods spending ~$500B above pre-COVID trend while services spending is ~$500B below; normalization will create significant fundamental headwinds

catalysts.event: Shift in consumer spending from goods to services post-Omicron
catalysts.impact: Reduces goods demand and semiconductor demand, accelerates inventory cycle unwind; benefits search advertising and services-oriented businesses; relieves supply-chain inflation

catalysts.event: Massive supply response in semiconductors and logistics coming online
catalysts.impact: Resolves supply-chain bottlenecks and goods inflation; TSMC 2021-2022 CapEx exceeds prior 5 years combined; Amazon spent more on CapEx in 2020-2021 than prior 20 years

catalysts.event: Economy slowing rapidly before Fed hikes even begin
catalysts.impact: Atlanta Fed GDP Now fell from 10 in November to 5 in December; retail sales flat in November, down 2 in December; pre-hike slowdown reduces inflation pressure and may limit Fed tightening cycle severity
JP Morgan announced panic to accelerate cloud migration; CIO surveys support this; likely to be best year for many cloud software companies
Could catalyze mainstream XR adoption and validate metaverse investment thesis; Apple's mobile OS control gives it structural advantage in XR platform
Lead times normalizing will cause customers to draw down inventories rapidly, creating demand that is significantly below true end demand; semiconductor companies will miss estimates significantly

meta_views.topic: Fed tightening cycles and equity returns
meta_views.view: Stocks are typically up 12 months after the first rate hike; market sells off into the hike because it is anticipatory
meta_views.detail: Baker cites data going back 25 years showing stocks up after first rate hike in every tightening cycle. The market is becoming more anticipatory over time. What is different this cycle is the 7% CPI print — the highest in 40 years — which is why the sell-off has been more severe than typical pre-hike corrections.

meta_views.view: Powell's reappointment was a sea change signal that the market correctly interpreted immediately as a mandate to crush inflation
meta_views.detail: Baker says he underreacted to Powell's reappointment and that it was a clear inflection point for markets that has persisted. He frames this as one of his mistakes.

meta_views.view: Even the Fed with more PhD economists, more data, and more computing power than anyone cannot forecast the economy beyond 6 months
meta_views.detail: Baker uses the 2010-2012 WSJ op-ed signed by most of the world's PhD economists predicting hyperinflation from QE (which never materialized) as a cautionary example. He applies this humility to all his macro views.

meta_views.view: The 2001-2008 value outperformance was driven by China/BRICs demand impulse, not just multiple dynamics; no comparable impulse exists today
meta_views.detail: Baker argues people misattribute value's post-bubble outperformance to multiple dynamics alone, missing that a massive fundamental earnings impulse from China's industrialization drove commodity and industrial earnings. Without a comparable demand impulse, a sustained value rotation is unlikely.

meta_views.topic: Private vs public market competitive dynamics
meta_views.view: Public equity investing is the most competitive arena imaginable; private investing requires different skills (access, rapport, process, value-add)
meta_views.detail: Baker describes public markets as the 'terror dome' where hundreds of millions of people compete to price equities. He values his 15 years of public-only experience as foundational. Key differentiator for public equity talent: ability to be rational when wrong. Key differentiator for private: ability to build founder rapport and add operational value.

meta_views.view: The venture industry is consolidating into four buckets: angels, seed-through-B specialists, large multi-stage crossover firms, and everyone else will struggle
meta_views.detail: Baker argues competitive pressure is forcing all serious venture firms to become crossover investors (Sequoia cited as example). Multi-stage crossover firms have a superior value proposition to founders (can own for 20 years, anchor IPOs). Seed-through-B specialists survive by being credentializing agencies and adding genuine value. Angels replace traditional board value-add.

meta_views.view: AI training's exponential compute requirements (quality doubles with every 10x increase in training data) have permanently shifted semiconductor industry growth rate upward
meta_views.detail: Baker cites a Microsoft research paper from ~10 years prior establishing the 10x data = 2x quality rule. Combined with Mark Andreessen's 'software eating the world' thesis evolving to 'AI eating software,' this creates a structural step-up in semiconductor demand intensity.

meta_views.view: AI has broken the historical ceiling on cybersecurity company scale by making scale an advantage rather than a liability for the first time
meta_views.detail: Previously, cybersecurity success attracted optimized attacks and VC-funded competitors, making it impossible to sustain companies above $15-20B market cap. AI-driven security reverses this dynamic because learning models benefit from scale, allowing companies to break through the historical ceiling.
